answer:
**Manu’s *Manusmṛti* (Book 1) frames the present age as the Kali Yuga, the final and most degenerate phase of a descending four-yuga cycle (Kṛta → Tretā → Dvāpara → Kali), each successively shorter and marked by declining dharma (righteousness), lifespan, and moral capacity.[^1] This cycle repeats endlessly within larger cosmic structures: a "day of Brahma" equals 1,000 divine years (12,000 human years per full yuga-set), followed by an equal night of dissolution and re-creation; 71 such sets form a manvantara (age of a Manu), with 14 manvantaras per Brahma-day.[^2] The text locates the present explicitly in Kali, where dharma stands on one foot, austerity yields to charity, and lifespans are shortest (~100 years), amid theft, falsehood, and malice—part of Brahma's eternal waking/sleeping rhythm of vivification and destruction.[^3]

**Cynewulf's poems (*Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*) depict the present as an interim on a singular, irreversible arc of salvation history: God's initial creation of "heaven and earth" and all therein, Christ's incarnation and victory over sin/death, ongoing spiritual warfare/mission, culminating in a final, apocalyptic Judgment Day ("doomsday") where creation collapses ("heaven and earth shall fall together"), souls are divided by deeds, and eternity begins.[^4] This is proclaimed repeatedly as linear teleology: e.g., Andreas recounts creation explicitly as God's singular crafting of cosmos and mankind, mirrored in Christ's miracles/recapitulation, pointing to eschaton; *Elene* echoes with creation-language and ends prophesying Judgment's tripartite fire-trial (righteous unscathed atop, middling purged, wicked abyssed); *Juliana* invokes Christ's rule "over all creation, forever, without end."[^5]

Cynewulf's history lacks any yuga-analogue—no descending moral epochs in cosmic repetition, no measured decline/reset within cycles. Implicit "decline" (heathen persecution, devil's temptations) serves linear progress toward Judgment, not recirculation.[^6] It is irreducibly linear: one Creation (past), Incarnation/present mission, one End (future parousia/dissolution/judgment/reward), echoing biblical protology-eschatology unity without Hindu-style eternal recurrence.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [paras. 68-70](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-a22081).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [paras. 71-80](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-5a3053).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 1, [paras. 81-86](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/01/translation/#p-fcafde).
[^4]: *Andreas*, Ch. 1, [para. e5fbef](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-e5fbef); *Elene*, Ch. 1, [paras. dcbbfe-8f8a1b](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-dcbbfe).
[^5]: *Andreas*, Ch. 1, [paras. 355089, 6384bb, bb7698](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-355089); *Juliana*, Ch. 1, [para. 6a2534](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-6a2534).
[^6]: E.g., *Elene*, Ch. 1, [paras. f5d1f1, 222dfa](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).

"fates of the apostles" OR christ cynewulf
doom OR judgment OR judgement OR dom OR world end OR creation OR beginning OR end cynewulf
